schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the laws of sexual holiness: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now to give the laws of forbidden relations and sexual holiness (the incest laws, the abominations of Egypt and Canaan, ch. 18, the speaking, God's command, the new section, the occasion, after the Day of Atonement and the law of the blood, now the laws of holiness in the most intimate relations, the purity of the family and the marriage bed, God himself giving these laws, showing the holiness that reaches into the most intimate human relations, the family and the marriage, guarded by his express command against the corruptions of the nations); the LORD speaking to Moses (the laws of holiness in the family) — the new law (the laws against the abominations, the purity of the intimate relations); the LORD spake unto Moses — the laws of sexual holiness introduced (the providence, God giving the laws of the intimate relations, the holiness reaching into the family) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the laws of sexual holiness
God giving the laws of forbidden relations and intimate purity (ch. 18)
the holiness that guards the family and the marriage against the corruptions of the nations

schools.summary: The charge grounded in 'I am the LORD your God': 'speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, I am the LORD your God' — the laws of holiness grounded in the great declaration 'I am the LORD your God,' the covenant name and relation (the I am the LORD your God, the ground of all the commands, the covenant relation, the authority and the motive, the figure, the holiness commanded because of who God is and his relation to his people, the obedience grounded in the covenant, 'I am the LORD your God' repeated through the chapter as the seal of every command, Leviticus 19:2); the command to all Israel (the laws for the whole people) — the address (the holiness required of all); 'I am the LORD your God' (the covenant name and relation, the ground of obedience, the authority and motive, the figure, the holiness grounded in who God is, the obedience flowing from the covenant relation, Leviticus 19:2, the providence, the laws sealed by the divine name, the holiness commanded) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, I am the LORD your God
the charge grounded in 'I am the LORD your God'
the holiness commanded because of who God is and his relation to his people

schools.summary: The warning against the doings of Egypt and Canaan: 'after the doings of the land of Egypt, wherein ye dwelt, shall ye not do: and after the doings of the land of Canaan, whither I bring you, shall ye not do: neither shall ye walk in their ordinances' — Israel not to follow the corrupt practices of Egypt (where they had dwelt) or Canaan (where they were going), nor walk in their customs (the doings of Egypt, the corruptions of the land they left, the doings of Canaan, the corruptions of the land they were entering, the walk in their ordinances, their customs and practices, the figure, the separation from the corruptions of the world on both sides, the people called out of the world's abominations and warned against them, 'be not conformed to this world,' Romans 12:2, 'come out from among them, and be ye separate,' 2 Corinthians 6:17); the corruptions of Egypt forbidden (the land they left) — the separation (from the world's abominations); 'the doings of the land of Canaan, whither I bring you, shall ye not do: neither shall ye walk in their ordinances' (the corruptions of Canaan forbidden, the land they were entering, the figure, the separation from the world on both sides, Romans 12:2; 2 Corinthians 6:17, the providence, the people warned against the abominations of the nations) — Clarke, Gill, Wesley.

themes: After the doings of the land of Egypt, wherein ye dwelt, shall ye not do: and after the doings of the land of Canaan, whither I bring you, shall ye not do: neither shall ye walk in their ordinances
the warning against the doings of Egypt and Canaan
the separation from the corruptions of the world on both sides
the people called out of the world's abominations (Romans 12:2; 2 Corinthians 6:17)

schools.summary: The charge to keep God's judgments and ordinances: 'ye shall do my judgments, and keep mine ordinances, to walk therein: I am the LORD your God' — instead of the nations' customs, Israel to do God's judgments and keep his ordinances and walk in them (the do my judgments, the obedience to God's law, the keep mine ordinances, the careful observance, the walk therein, the manner of life, the I am the LORD your God, the ground, the figure, the obedience to God's law in place of the world's customs, the walk in God's ways, the positive holiness that replaces the renounced corruptions, 'walk in the Spirit, and ye shall not fulfil the lust of the flesh,' Galatians 5:16); the keeping of God's judgments and ordinances (the obedience, the walk in God's ways) — the holiness (the positive obedience); 'I am the LORD your God' (the covenant ground, the authority and motive, vv.2, the figure, the obedience grounded in the covenant God, Galatians 5:16, the providence, the laws sealed by the divine name) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: Ye shall do my judgments, and keep mine ordinances, to walk therein: I am the LORD your God
the charge to keep God's judgments and ordinances
the obedience to God's law in place of the world's customs, the walk in God's ways
the positive holiness that replaces the renounced corruptions (Galatians 5:16)

schools.summary: The promise of life in keeping the law: 'ye shall therefore keep my statutes, and my judgments: which if a man do, he shall live in them: I am the LORD' — doing and living — 'which if a man do, he shall live in them': the man who keeps God's statutes shall live in them (the witnesses noting the significance — the principle of the law, the law promising life to the one who does it, 'the man which doeth those things shall live by them,' the lesson — the great Pauline crux: Paul cites this verse for the principle of the law as a way of righteousness, 'Moses describeth the righteousness which is of the law, That the man which doeth those things shall live by them,' Romans 10:5, 'the law is not of faith: but, The man that doeth them shall live in them,' Galatians 3:12, contrasting it with the righteousness of faith, the law says 'do and live,' but since no man can perfectly do, it cannot give life to the sinner, 'for if there had been a law given which could have given life, verily righteousness should have been by the law,' Galatians 3:21, the lesson — the law's holy demand of perfect obedience, which condemns the sinner who cannot keep it, and which is fulfilled only in Christ, who alone perfectly did and lived, and gives his righteousness to those who believe, Romans 10:4, 'Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth,' some among the witnesses noting also the temporal sense, long life and prosperity in the land for obedience, and the higher promise of life eternal in the obedience of faith that Christ supplies, the figure — the law of doing and living, holy and good, yet unable to justify the sinner, driving him to Christ who is our life); the keeping of the statutes (the obedience required, with the promise of life, the temporal and the higher sense) — the obedience (the law's demand, the figure, the doing that the law requires, fulfilled in Christ); 'I am the LORD' (the covenant authority, the seal of the command, the figure, the law grounded in God, holy and good, Romans 7:12, the providence, the law of doing and living, pointing to Christ, Romans 10:4-5; Galatians 3:12, 21) — Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'which if a man do, he shall live in them' teach?

sides.position: The law's principle of life by doing (Romans 10:5; Galatians 3:12), holy and good yet unable to justify the sinner who cannot perfectly keep it (Galatians 3:21), driving him to Christ who alone did and lived and is the end of the law for righteousness (Romans 10:4) - some also noting the temporal sense of long life in the land

themes: Ye shall therefore keep my statutes, and my judgments: which if a man do, he shall live in them: I am the LORD
the promise of life in keeping the law (the crux of doing and living)
the law's principle of life by doing, holy and good yet unable to justify the sinner (Romans 10:5; Galatians 3:12, 21)
driving him to Christ who alone did and lived and is the end of the law for righteousness (Romans 10:4)

schools.summary: The general law against approaching near of kin: 'None of you shall approach to any that is near of kin to him, to uncover their nakedness: I am the LORD' — the general prohibition — 'None of you shall approach to any that is near of kin to him, to uncover their nakedness': the general law forbidding sexual relations with near kindred, the foundation of the incest prohibitions that follow (vv.7-18, the witnesses noting the significance — the general principle, no one to approach a near relation to uncover their nakedness, the euphemism for sexual union, the lesson — the sanctity of the family and the bounds God sets on the marriage relation, the natural and holy bounds of kinship, within which marriage is forbidden, the family relations to be kept pure and the reverence due to blood, the incest laws guarding the sanctity of the family, the honor of parents and kindred, the purity of the home, the basis — some noting the law written on nature, the natural reverence and shame that fence the family, confirmed and defined by God's express command, the figure — the holiness that guards the most intimate bonds, the family as a sacred order God has set, the bounds of kinship within which the marriage union is forbidden, the reverence and purity due to the near of kin, and the seal 'I am the LORD' — the authority of God who sets these bounds, his holiness reaching into the family and the marriage bed); the uncovering of nakedness (the euphemism for sexual union, forbidden with the near of kin) — the prohibition (the incest forbidden, the family guarded, the figure, the sanctity of the kindred, the bounds of marriage); 'I am the LORD' (the authority of God, who sets the bounds of the family, the figure, the holiness reaching into the family, the reverence due to the near of kin, the providence, the general law, the particular cases to follow, vv.7-18) — Calvin, Keil, Henry, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the law against approaching near of kin establish?

sides.position: The general foundation of the incest prohibitions - the sanctity of the family and the bounds God sets on the marriage relation (the natural reverence and purity due to blood, the family a sacred order, the honor of parents and kindred), the holiness that guards the most intimate bonds, sealed by 'I am the LORD'

themes: None of you shall approach to any that is near of kin to him, to uncover their nakedness: I am the LORD
the general law against approaching near of kin (the crux of the sanctity of the family)
the foundation of the incest prohibitions, the holiness that guards the family
the bounds God sets on the marriage relation, the reverence and purity due to the near of kin

schools.summary: The prohibition against the mother: 'the nakedness of thy father, or the nakedness of thy mother, shalt thou not uncover: she is thy mother; thou shalt not uncover her nakedness' — the first and most sacred case: the son not to uncover the nakedness of his mother (which is also his father's nakedness, the dishonor of both, the nakedness of thy father, the father's shame, the mother being his, the she is thy mother, the ground, the reverence due to the mother, the figure, the first and most fundamental bound, the honor due to father and mother, the reverence of the parent, the foundation of the family honor, 'honour thy father and thy mother,' Exodus 20:12, the sin of dishonoring the parent, as the great wickedness of those who later defiled the father's bed, 1 Corinthians 5:1); the prohibition against the mother (the father's nakedness being hers) — the reverence (the honor of the parents); 'she is thy mother; thou shalt not uncover her nakedness' (the reverence due to the mother, the ground of the prohibition, the figure, the honor of the parent, Exodus 20:12; 1 Corinthians 5:1, the providence, the first bound, the cases to follow) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: The nakedness of thy father, or the nakedness of thy mother, shalt thou not uncover: she is thy mother; thou shalt not uncover her nakedness
the prohibition against the mother
the first and most sacred bound, the reverence due to the parent (Exodus 20:12)
the foundation of the family honor (1 Corinthians 5:1)

schools.summary: The prohibition against the father's wife: 'the nakedness of thy father's wife shalt thou not uncover: it is thy father's nakedness' — the son not to uncover the nakedness of his father's wife (the stepmother), for it was his father's nakedness (his father's shame and dishonor, the father's wife, the stepmother, not the natural mother but the father's other wife, the it is thy father's nakedness, the dishonor of the father, the figure, the honor of the father guarded, the father's bed sacred, this the very sin Paul condemns in the Corinthian church, 'that one should have his father's wife,' 1 Corinthians 5:1, a wickedness not named even among the Gentiles, the reverence due to the father's marriage); the prohibition against the father's wife (the stepmother) — the honor (the father's bed guarded); 'it is thy father's nakedness' (the father's shame, the dishonor of the father, the figure, the honor of the father, the sin of 1 Corinthians 5:1, the providence, the father's wife forbidden, the cases continuing) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: The nakedness of thy father's wife shalt thou not uncover: it is thy father's nakedness
the prohibition against the father's wife
the honor of the father guarded, the father's bed sacred
the very sin Paul condemns as not named even among the Gentiles (1 Corinthians 5:1)

schools.summary: The prohibition against the sister: 'the nakedness of thy sister, the daughter of thy father, or daughter of thy mother, whether she be born at home, or born abroad, even their nakedness thou shalt not uncover' — the brother not to uncover the nakedness of his sister, whether full sister or half (daughter of father or mother), whether born in the house or elsewhere (the daughter of thy father or mother, full or half sister, the born at home or abroad, whether legitimate or otherwise, in or out of the house, the figure, the sanctity of the sister, the bound of the family extended to the siblings, the reverence due to the sister, the purity of the family relations, the sin reproved in such unions, as Tamar pleaded with Amnon, 2 Samuel 13:12-13); the prohibition against the sister (full or half) — the bound (the family extended to siblings); 'whether she be born at home, or born abroad, even their nakedness thou shalt not uncover' (the sister in every case, born in or out of the house, the figure, the sanctity of the sister, 2 Samuel 13:12-13, the providence, the sister forbidden, the cases continuing) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: The nakedness of thy sister, the daughter of thy father, or daughter of thy mother, whether she be born at home, or born abroad, even their nakedness thou shalt not uncover
the prohibition against the sister
the bound of the family extended to the siblings, the reverence due to the sister
the purity of the family relations (2 Samuel 13:12-13)

schools.summary: The prohibition against the granddaughter: 'the nakedness of thy son's daughter, or of thy daughter's daughter, even their nakedness thou shalt not uncover: for theirs is thine own nakedness' — the grandfather not to uncover the nakedness of his granddaughter (by son or daughter), for it was his own nakedness (his own flesh and blood, the son's daughter or daughter's daughter, the granddaughter, either line, the theirs is thine own nakedness, the granddaughter being his own flesh, the figure, the bound extended to the grandchild, the reverence of the descending line, the granddaughter as one's own flesh, the sanctity of the family through the generations, the purity guarded down the line of descent); the prohibition against the granddaughter (either line) — the bound (the descending line); 'for theirs is thine own nakedness' (the granddaughter being his own flesh, the ground, the figure, the family through the generations, the own flesh guarded, the providence, the granddaughter forbidden, the cases continuing) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: The nakedness of thy son's daughter, or of thy daughter's daughter, even their nakedness thou shalt not uncover: for theirs is thine own nakedness
the prohibition against the granddaughter
the bound extended to the grandchild, the granddaughter as one's own flesh
the sanctity of the family through the generations, the purity guarded down the line of descent

schools.summary: The prohibition against the stepsister begotten of the father: 'the nakedness of thy father's wife's daughter, begotten of thy father, she is thy sister, thou shalt not uncover her nakedness' — the daughter of the father's wife, begotten of the father (the half sister by the father), also forbidden, for she is a sister (the father's wife's daughter, begotten of thy father, the half sister by the father, born of the stepmother, the she is thy sister, the ground, the sister relation, the figure, the bound extended to the half sister by the father, the sister relation guarded however the kinship arises, the sanctity of the sibling relation, the purity of the family); the prohibition against the father's wife's daughter (begotten of the father, the half sister) — the bound (the sister relation); 'she is thy sister, thou shalt not uncover her nakedness' (the sister relation, the ground of the prohibition, the figure, the sanctity of the sister, vv.9, the providence, the half sister forbidden, the cases continuing) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: The nakedness of thy father's wife's daughter, begotten of thy father, she is thy sister, thou shalt not uncover her nakedness

the bound extended to the half sister by the father, the sister relation guarded

schools.summary: The prohibition against the father's sister: 'thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy father's sister: she is thy father's near kinswoman' — the father's sister (the paternal aunt) forbidden, for she is the father's near kinswoman (his blood, the father's sister, the paternal aunt, the she is thy father's near kinswoman, the ground, the father's blood relation, the figure, the bound extended to the aunt, the reverence of the parent's kindred, the aunt as the father's near flesh, the sanctity of the family extended to the parents' siblings, the purity guarded in the wider kindred); the prohibition against the father's sister (the paternal aunt) — the bound (the parent's kindred); 'she is thy father's near kinswoman' (the father's near blood, the ground, the figure, the aunt as the father's near flesh, the wider kindred guarded, the providence, the paternal aunt forbidden, the maternal aunt to follow, vv.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy father's sister: she is thy father's near kinswoman

schools.summary: The prohibition against the mother's sister: 'thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy mother's sister: for she is thy mother's near kinswoman' — the mother's sister (the maternal aunt) likewise forbidden, for she is the mother's near kinswoman (the mother's sister, the maternal aunt, the she is thy mother's near kinswoman, the ground, the mother's blood relation, the figure, the bound extended to the maternal aunt as to the paternal, vv.12, the reverence of both parents' kindred, the purity guarded in the wider family on both sides); the prohibition against the mother's sister (the maternal aunt) — the bound (the parent's kindred, both sides); 'for she is thy mother's near kinswoman' (the mother's near blood, the ground, the figure, the aunt as the mother's near flesh, the wider kindred on both sides, the providence, the maternal aunt forbidden, the cases continuing) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy mother's sister: for she is thy mother's near kinswoman
the prohibition against the mother's sister

the reverence of both parents' kindred, the purity guarded in the wider family

schools.summary: The prohibition against the uncle's wife: 'thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy father's brother, thou shalt not approach to his wife: she is thine aunt' — the wife of the father's brother (the uncle by marriage) forbidden, for she is the man's aunt (by marriage, the father's brother, the uncle, the his wife: she is thine aunt, the aunt by marriage, forbidden as the uncle's nakedness, the figure, the bound extended to the aunt by marriage, the uncle's wife guarded as the uncle's own nakedness, the affinity as well as the blood relation honored, the reverence of the uncle's marriage); the prohibition reaching the uncle's nakedness (through his wife) — the bound (the affinity); 'thou shalt not approach to his wife: she is thine aunt' (the uncle's wife, the aunt by marriage, forbidden, the figure, the affinity honored, the uncle's marriage guarded, the providence, the uncle's wife forbidden, the cases continuing) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy father's brother, thou shalt not approach to his wife: she is thine aunt

schools.summary: The prohibition against the daughter-in-law: 'thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy daughter in law: she is thy son's wife; thou shalt not uncover her nakedness' — the daughter-in-law (the son's wife) forbidden, for she is the son's wife (the daughter in law, the son's wife, the she is thy son's wife, the ground, the affinity through the son, the figure, the bound extended to the daughter-in-law, the son's marriage guarded, the very sin of Judah with Tamar reproved, though in ignorance, Genesis 38:18, 26, the reverence of the son's bed, the affinity honored); the prohibition against the daughter-in-law (the son's wife) — the bound (the affinity through the son); 'she is thy son's wife; thou shalt not uncover her nakedness' (the son's wife, the ground, the affinity, the figure, the son's bed guarded, Genesis 38:18, 26, the providence, the daughter-in-law forbidden, the cases continuing) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy daughter in law: she is thy son's wife; thou shalt not uncover her nakedness
the prohibition against the daughter-in-law
the bound extended to the daughter-in-law, the son's bed guarded
the affinity honored (Genesis 38:18, 26)

schools.summary: The prohibition against the brother's wife (with the levirate exception): 'thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy brother's wife: it is thy brother's nakedness' — the prohibition — 'thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy brother's wife: it is thy brother's nakedness': the brother's wife forbidden, for she is the brother's nakedness (the witnesses noting the significance — the general prohibition, the brother's wife forbidden, the brother's bed guarded as his own nakedness, the noted exception — most of the witnesses observing the levirate exception of Deuteronomy 25:5: when a brother died childless, his brother to take the widow to raise up seed to the dead, an exception that proves the general rule, the brother's wife forbidden except in the levirate duty to perpetuate the dead brother's name, the application — this the law John the Baptist pressed on Herod, who had taken his brother Philip's wife, 'it is not lawful for thee to have thy brother's wife,' Mark 6:18, the figure — the sanctity of the brother's marriage, the brother's bed guarded, with the gracious levirate provision for the childless dead, the reverence due to the brother); the brother's nakedness (the brother dishonored, his bed guarded), with the levirate exception of Deuteronomy 25:5 (the widow taken to raise seed to the dead) — the figure (the brother's marriage honored, the levirate provision); the general prohibition with the levirate exception (Deuteronomy 25:5), and the application to Herod (Mark 6:18) — the providence (the brother's wife forbidden save in the levirate duty) — Calvin, Wesley, Clarke, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What is the relation of the prohibition to the levirate law?

sides.position: The brother's wife is forbidden (the brother's bed guarded as his own nakedness), with the gracious exception of the levirate duty for the childless dead (Deuteronomy 25:5, raising up seed to the dead brother) - the law John the Baptist pressed on Herod (Mark 6:18)

themes: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy brother's wife: it is thy brother's nakedness
the prohibition against the brother's wife (the crux with the levirate exception)
the brother's bed guarded as his own nakedness, with the levirate provision for the childless dead (Deuteronomy 25:5)
the law John the Baptist pressed on Herod (Mark 6:18)

schools.summary: The prohibition against a woman and her daughter or granddaughter: 'thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of a woman and her daughter, neither shalt thou take her son's daughter, or her daughter's daughter, to uncover her nakedness; for they are her near kinswomen: it is wickedness' — a man not to take both a woman and her daughter or granddaughter, for they are near kin to one another, and it is wickedness (the a woman and her daughter, both forbidden together, the her son's daughter or daughter's daughter, the granddaughter also, the they are her near kinswomen, the ground, the near kinship, the it is wickedness, the strong condemnation, lewdness, the figure, the bound guarding against the confusion of the generations, the near kin of the wife also forbidden, the strong word 'wickedness' marking the gravity, the corruption of the family order); the woman and her daughter forbidden together (the near kin) — the bound (the confusion of generations guarded); 'for they are her near kinswomen: it is wickedness' (the near kinship, the ground, and the strong condemnation, wickedness, lewdness, the figure, the gravity of the corruption, the family order guarded, the providence, the wickedness forbidden, the cases continuing) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of a woman and her daughter, neither shalt thou take her son's daughter, or her daughter's daughter, to uncover her nakedness; for they are her near kinswomen: it is wickedness
the prohibition against a woman and her daughter or granddaughter
the bound against the confusion of the generations, the near kin of the wife forbidden
the strong word 'wickedness' marking the gravity, the corruption of the family order

schools.summary: The prohibition against marrying a wife's sister in her lifetime: 'neither shalt thou take a wife to her sister, to vex her, to uncover her nakedness, beside the other in her life time' — the prohibition — 'neither shalt thou take a wife to her sister, to vex her... beside the other in her life time': a man not to marry his wife's sister during the wife's lifetime, to be a rival and vexation to her (the witnesses noting the significance — the prohibition of marrying two sisters at once, the wife's sister forbidden while the wife lived, to prevent the rivalry and vexation between sisters, the lesson — the warning against the rivalry and strife that such a union breeds, the example of Jacob with Leah and Rachel, the rival sisters whose contention vexed the household, Genesis 30:1, 8, 14-16, the in her life time, the limit, suggesting that after the wife's death the sister might be taken, as most read it, the debate — some reading this as a general condemnation of polygamy, 'a wife to her sister' understood as 'one wife to another,' forbidding the taking of rival wives, but most reading it specifically of the wife's sister, the figure — the peace and order of the household guarded, the rivalry and vexation forbidden, the wisdom of God protecting the marriage from strife); the vexation (the rivalry between sisters, the strife of the divided household, Genesis 30:1) — the warning (against the contention such a union breeds); the limit 'in her life time' (the sister forbidden while the wife lived, permitted after her death by most readings), and the debate over the general-polygamy reading — the providence (the household peace guarded) — Calvin, Wesley, Clarke, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: Does this forbid the wife's sister specifically, or polygamy generally?

sides.position: Most read it specifically: a man may not marry his wife's sister during the wife's lifetime, to prevent rivalry and vexation between sisters (the example of Leah and Rachel, Genesis 30:1) - with the limit 'in her life time' permitting the sister after the wife's death
Some read 'a wife to her sister' as a general condemnation of polygamy, forbidding the taking of rival wives
holders: a minority reading noted by the witnesses

themes: Neither shalt thou take a wife to her sister, to vex her, to uncover her nakedness, beside the other in her life time
the prohibition against marrying a wife's sister in her lifetime (the crux on rival sisters)
the rivalry and vexation forbidden, the peace of the household guarded (Genesis 30:1)
the noted limit 'in her life time' and the debate over the general-polygamy reading

schools.summary: The prohibition against approaching the separated woman: 'also thou shalt not approach unto a woman to uncover her nakedness, as long as she is put apart for her uncleanness' — a man not to approach a woman in her menstrual separation (Leviticus 15:19-24, the as long as she is put apart for her uncleanness, the time of her separation, the figure, the reverence of the natural order, the bound set even within marriage, the self-control and honor that the law teaches even in the lawful relation, the respect for the woman's seasons, the holiness that governs even the marriage bed, 'possess his vessel in sanctification and honour,' 1 Thessalonians 4:4, the gravity later marked, that to do so is to be cut off, Leviticus 20:18); the woman in her separation forbidden (Leviticus 15:19) — the bound (even within marriage); 'as long as she is put apart for her uncleanness' (the time of her separation, the bound set, the figure, the self-control and honor in the marriage, 1 Thessalonians 4:4, the gravity, Leviticus 20:18, the providence, the bound honored, the holiness of the marriage) — Henry, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Also thou shalt not approach unto a woman to uncover her nakedness, as long as she is put apart for her uncleanness
the prohibition against approaching the separated woman
the reverence of the natural order, the bound set even within marriage (Leviticus 15:19)
the self-control and honor the law teaches even in the lawful relation (1 Thessalonians 4:4)

schools.summary: The prohibition against adultery: 'moreover thou shalt not lie carnally with thy neighbour's wife, to defile thyself with her' — adultery forbidden, the lying with the neighbour's wife, which defiles (the neighbour's wife, the married woman, not one's own, the to defile thyself with her, the defilement of adultery, the figure, the seventh commandment, 'thou shalt not commit adultery,' Exodus 20:14, the sanctity of marriage and the neighbour's bed guarded, the defilement of adultery, 'marriage is honourable in all, and the bed undefiled: but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge,' Hebrews 13:4, the gravity of the sin that defiles both the adulterer and the marriage); the prohibition of adultery (the neighbour's wife, the seventh commandment, Exodus 20:14) — the sanctity (of marriage and the neighbour's bed); 'to defile thyself with her' (the defilement of adultery, the adulterer and the marriage defiled, the figure, the sin that defiles, Hebrews 13:4, the providence, the adultery forbidden, the abominations continuing) — Calvin, Keil, Gill.

themes: Moreover thou shalt not lie carnally with thy neighbour's wife, to defile thyself with her
the prohibition against adultery
the seventh commandment, the sanctity of marriage and the neighbour's bed guarded (Exodus 20:14)
the defilement of adultery condemned (Hebrews 13:4)

schools.summary: The prohibition of passing seed through the fire to Molech: 'and thou shalt not let any of thy seed pass through the fire to Molech, neither shalt thou profane the name of thy God: I am the LORD' — the Molech worship — 'thou shalt not let any of thy seed pass through the fire to Molech': the dreadful idolatry of Molech, the passing of children through (or in) the fire, forbidden (the witnesses noting the significance — the worship of Molech, the Ammonite idol to whom children were offered, made to pass through the fire, whether as a dedication or as a burnt sacrifice, the lesson — the abomination of child sacrifice, the unnatural cruelty and idolatry combined, the parents offering their own children to the fire, the most dreadful of the heathen abominations, 'they have built also the high places of Baal, to burn their sons with fire for burnt offerings unto Baal,' Jeremiah 19:5, 'they sacrificed their sons and their daughters unto devils,' Psalm 106:37-38, the profaning of God's name — to give one's seed to Molech was to profane the name of God, to dishonor him by giving to the idol the children who belonged to him, the lesson — the placing of this abomination among the sexual sins, perhaps because the worship of Molech was bound up with the corruptions of the heathen, or because giving one's seed to the idol corrupts the very fruit of the marriage, the figure — the dreadful idolatry that sacrifices the children, the profaning of God's holy name, judged most severely, Leviticus 20:2-5, the worshipper of Molech to be stoned, and the seal 'I am the LORD' — the holiness of God against this abomination); the profaning of God's name (to give the seed to Molech dishonors God, the name profaned by the idolatry) — the dishonor (the idolatry profaning the holy name); the seal 'I am the LORD' (the holiness of God against the Molech worship, the severe judgment, Leviticus 20:2-5) — the providence (the abomination forbidden, judged most severely) — Calvin, Wesley, Clarke, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does the prohibition of Molech worship teach?

sides.position: The dreadful idolatry that passes the children through the fire, the most unnatural of the heathen abominations (child sacrifice, Jeremiah 19:5; Psalm 106:37-38) - the profaning of God's name by giving his children to the idol, judged most severely (Leviticus 20:2-5)

themes: And thou shalt not let any of thy seed pass through the fire to Molech, neither shalt thou profane the name of thy God: I am the LORD
the prohibition of passing seed through the fire to Molech (the crux of child sacrifice)
the dreadful idolatry of child sacrifice, the most unnatural of the heathen abominations (Jeremiah 19:5; Psalm 106:37-38)
the profaning of God's name, judged most severely (Leviticus 20:2-5)

schools.summary: The prohibition of lying with mankind: 'thou shalt not lie with mankind, as with womankind: it is abomination' — the prohibition — 'thou shalt not lie with mankind, as with womankind: it is abomination': the homosexual act forbidden, and called by the strongest term, abomination (the witnesses noting the significance — the prohibition of the act between men, the lying with mankind as with womankind, against nature, the lesson — the strong word 'abomination' (toebah), marking it as a thing detestable to God, a perversion of the natural order God established in creating man and woman, Genesis 1:27; 2:24, the New Testament confirmation — Paul expounds this as the unnatural sin into which God gives up those who reject him, 'men with men working that which is unseemly,' 'the natural use' abandoned for that 'which is against nature,' Romans 1:26-27, and names such among those who shall not inherit the kingdom, 1 Corinthians 6:9-10, yet adds 'and such were some of you: but ye are washed... sanctified... justified,' 1 Corinthians 6:11, the grace that cleanses even this, the lesson — the perversion of the created order, the abomination against nature, named by God as detestable, the figure — the holiness of God against the corruption of the natural order, the sin called abomination, yet not beyond the cleansing of Christ for the penitent); the act against nature (the lying with mankind as with womankind, the perversion of the natural order, Romans 1:26-27) — the unnatural (the created order perverted); the strong word 'abomination' (toebah, the thing detestable to God, the gravity, 1 Corinthians 6:9-10) — the providence (the abomination forbidden, judged, yet the grace that cleanses the penitent, 1 Corinthians 6:11) — Calvin, Wesley, Clarke, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does the prohibition of lying with mankind teach?

sides.position: The perversion of the natural order God established (Genesis 1:27), named by the strongest term 'abomination,' confirmed by Paul as the unnatural sin (Romans 1:26-27) that bars from the kingdom (1 Corinthians 6:9-10) - yet not beyond the cleansing grace of Christ for the penitent (1 Corinthians 6:11)

themes: Thou shalt not lie with mankind, as with womankind: it is abomination
the prohibition of lying with mankind (the crux of the abomination of sodomy)
the perversion of the created order, named by God as abomination (Genesis 1:27; Romans 1:26-27)
that bars from the kingdom, yet not beyond the cleansing of Christ for the penitent (1 Corinthians 6:9-11)

schools.summary: The prohibition of bestiality: 'neither shalt thou lie with any beast to defile thyself therewith: neither shall any woman stand before a beast to lie down thereto: it is confusion' — bestiality, by man or woman, forbidden, and called confusion (the lie with any beast, the man, the any woman stand before a beast, the woman, the it is confusion, tebel, the perversion, the violent confounding of the order of creation, the figure, the abomination that confounds the order of nature, mixing man with beast, the strong word 'confusion' marking the violation of the created distinction between man and the animals, the deepest degradation, the perversion of the image of God in man, judged with death, Leviticus 20:15-16; Exodus 22:19); the prohibition of bestiality by the man (the defilement) — the abomination (the confounding of nature); 'neither shall any woman stand before a beast to lie down thereto: it is confusion' (the woman likewise forbidden, the act called confusion, the perversion of creation's order, the figure, the violation of the created distinction, the degradation of the image of God, Leviticus 20:15-16, the providence, the abomination forbidden, judged with death, Exodus 22:19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Neither shalt thou lie with any beast to defile thyself therewith: neither shall any woman stand before a beast to lie down thereto: it is confusion
the prohibition of bestiality
the abomination that confounds the order of nature, mixing man with beast
the violation of the created distinction, the deepest degradation, judged with death (Leviticus 20:15-16; Exodus 22:19)

schools.summary: The warning against the defilements that polluted the nations: 'defile not ye yourselves in any of these things: for in all these the nations are defiled which I cast out before you' — Israel not to defile themselves with any of these abominations, for the very nations God was casting out had defiled themselves with them (the defile not yourselves, the warning, the the nations are defiled, the Canaanites polluted by these very sins, the which I cast out before you, the nations being driven out for these abominations, the figure, the abominations that polluted the nations and brought God's judgment on them, the warning that these very sins were the cause of the Canaanites' destruction, the reason the land was being given to Israel, the holiness that cannot endure such defilement, 'the wickedness of the Amorites is not yet full,' Genesis 15:16); the warning against the defilements (the abominations forbidden) — the holiness (the people kept pure); 'the nations are defiled which I cast out before you' (the Canaanites polluted by these sins, the cause of their being cast out, Genesis 15:16, the figure, the abominations that brought God's judgment on the nations, the providence, the nations cast out for these sins, the land given to Israel) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: Defile not ye yourselves in any of these things: for in all these the nations are defiled which I cast out before you
the warning against the defilements that polluted the nations
the abominations that polluted the nations and brought God's judgment on them
the cause of the Canaanites' destruction (Genesis 15:16)

schools.summary: The land vomiting out its inhabitants: 'and the land is defiled: therefore I do visit the iniquity thereof upon it, and the land itself vomiteth out her inhabitants' — the vomiting land — 'the land is defiled... and the land itself vomiteth out her inhabitants': the land so defiled by these abominations that God visited its iniquity, and the land itself, as it were, vomited out its inhabitants (the witnesses noting the significance — the moral pollution of the land, the land defiled by the sins of its inhabitants, the very ground polluted, the lesson — the striking and solemn figure of the land vomiting out its inhabitants, as a body casts out poison, or a stomach rejects what is loathsome, the land could not retain a people so corrupt, but cast them out, the divine visitation — 'I do visit the iniquity thereof upon it,' God's judgment on the land for its sins, the iniquity visited, the Canaanites cast out, the lesson — that sin pollutes not only the sinner but the land and the place, that there is a moral order in the very creation that recoils against the corruption of those who dwell in it, the warning to Israel — that if they defiled the land likewise, it would vomit them out too, vv.28, as indeed it did in the exile, the figure — the moral pollution of the land and the judgment of God that casts out the corrupt, the solemn truth that the land itself cannot bear persistent abomination); God visiting the iniquity of the land (the judgment for the sins, the Canaanites cast out) — the visitation (the divine judgment on the defiled land); the striking figure of the land vomiting out its inhabitants (the land casting out the corrupt as loathsome, vv.28) — the figure (the moral order recoiling against corruption, the providence, the land casting out the defiled, the warning to Israel) — Calvin, Keil, Clarke, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the land vomiting out its inhabitants signify?

sides.position: The moral pollution of the land by the abominations of its inhabitants, the divine visitation, and the striking figure of the land casting out the corrupt as loathsome - a solemn warning that sin pollutes the very land and that the moral order recoils against persistent abomination (a warning Israel would prove in the exile, vv.28)

themes: And the land is defiled: therefore I do visit the iniquity thereof upon it, and the land itself vomiteth out her inhabitants
the land vomiting out its inhabitants (the crux of the moral pollution of the land)
the moral pollution of the land by abomination, the divine visitation
the land casting out the corrupt as loathsome, the warning that sin pollutes the very land (vv.28)

schools.summary: The charge to keep the statutes binding native and stranger: 'ye shall therefore keep my statutes and my judgments, and shall not commit any of these abominations; neither any of your own nation, nor any stranger that sojourneth among you' — Israel to keep God's statutes and commit none of these abominations, the law binding both the native Israelite and the stranger sojourning among them (the keep my statutes, the obedience required, the not commit any of these abominations, the prohibition, the neither native nor stranger, the law binding all in the land, the figure, the holiness required of all who dwell in the land, native and sojourner alike, the law of purity binding on all, the universality of the moral law, which binds the stranger as well as the Israelite, Numbers 15:16); the keeping of the statutes and the avoiding of the abominations (the obedience and the purity) — the holiness (the people kept pure); 'neither any of your own nation, nor any stranger that sojourneth among you' (the law binding native and stranger, all in the land, the figure, the universality of the moral law, Numbers 15:16, the providence, the law binding all, the land kept pure) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Ye shall therefore keep my statutes and my judgments, and shall not commit any of these abominations; neither any of your own nation, nor any stranger that sojourneth among you
the charge to keep the statutes binding native and stranger
the holiness required of all who dwell in the land, native and sojourner alike
the universality of the moral law, binding the stranger as well as the Israelite (Numbers 15:16)

schools.summary: The reason: the abominations that defiled the land: '(for all these abominations have the men of the land done, which were before you, and the land is defiled;)' — the reason for the warning: the former inhabitants (the Canaanites) had done all these abominations, and the land was defiled by them (the the men of the land done, the Canaanites' abominations, the which were before you, the former inhabitants, the the land is defiled, the pollution of the land, vv.25, the figure, the abominations that defiled the land and brought judgment on the nations, the reason for the warning and the casting out, the land polluted by the sins of its inhabitants, the moral defilement that demanded the judgment); the Canaanites' abominations (the former inhabitants, the cause of their judgment) — the defilement (the sins that polluted the land); 'and the land is defiled' (the land polluted by the sins, vv.25, the figure, the moral pollution that demanded judgment, the providence, the land defiled, the casting out to follow, vv.28) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: (For all these abominations have the men of the land done, which were before you, and the land is defiled;)
the reason: the abominations that defiled the land
the abominations that polluted the land and brought judgment on the nations (vv.25)
the reason for the warning and the casting out

schools.summary: The warning that the land would vomit out Israel too: 'that the land spue not you out also, when ye defile it, as it spued out the nations that were before you' — the warning: if Israel defiled the land as the nations had, the land would vomit them out too, as it had vomited the Canaanites (the spue not you out also, the same judgment threatened on Israel, the when ye defile it, the condition, if they defiled it likewise, the as it spued out the nations, the precedent, the Canaanites cast out, vv.25, the figure, the solemn warning that Israel was not exempt — the same moral order that cast out the nations would cast out Israel if they sinned likewise, the land vomiting out the defilers whoever they be, a warning fulfilled in Israel's later exile, 'ye shall soon utterly perish from off the land,' Deuteronomy 4:26; 2 Kings 17:20); the warning to Israel (the land would cast them out too if they defiled it) — the warning (Israel not exempt); 'as it spued out the nations that were before you' (the Canaanites cast out, the precedent, vv.25, the figure, the same judgment threatened on Israel, fulfilled in the exile, Deuteronomy 4:26; 2 Kings 17:20, the providence, the land vomiting out the defilers, Israel warned) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: That the land spue not you out also, when ye defile it, as it spued out the nations that were before you
the warning that the land would vomit out Israel too
the solemn warning that Israel was not exempt, the land vomiting out the defilers whoever they be
fulfilled in Israel's later exile (Deuteronomy 4:26; 2 Kings 17:20)

schools.summary: The penalty of being cut off for the abominations: 'for whosoever shall commit any of these abominations, even the souls that commit them shall be cut off from among their people' — whoever committed any of these abominations to be cut off from among the people (the whosoever, any who commit them, the the souls that commit them, the persons themselves, the cut off from among their people, the penalty, excision, whether by death or by being cut off from the covenant people, divine or judicial, the figure, the gravity of the abominations, the severe penalty of cutting off, the soul that sins to be cut off, the holiness that purges the abomination from among the people, the warning of the wages of such sin, Romans 6:23); the ones who commit the abominations (any who do them) — the penalty (the cutting off); 'the souls that commit them shall be cut off from among their people' (the penalty of excision, cut off from the people, by death or divine judgment, the figure, the holiness purging the abomination, the wages of sin, Romans 6:23, the providence, the offenders cut off, the people purged) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: For whosoever shall commit any of these abominations, even the souls that commit them shall be cut off from among their people
the penalty of being cut off for the abominations
the gravity of the abominations, the severe penalty of cutting off
the holiness that purges the abomination from among the people (Romans 6:23)

schools.summary: The closing charge to keep God's ordinance and not defile themselves: 'therefore shall ye keep mine ordinance, that ye commit not any one of these abominable customs, which were committed before you, and that ye defile not yourselves therein: I am the LORD your God' — the closing charge to keep God's ordinance, commit none of the abominable customs of the nations, and not defile themselves, sealed by 'I am the LORD your God' (the keep mine ordinance, the obedience required, the not commit any one of these abominable customs, the prohibition of all the abominations, the defile not yourselves therein, the purity required, the I am the LORD your God, the covenant ground and seal, vv.2, the figure, the holiness required, the people kept pure from the abominable customs of the nations, the obedience grounded in the covenant God, the keeping of God's ordinance the way of holiness, the seal of the divine name closing the chapter, 'be ye holy; for I am holy,' 1 Peter 1:16); the keeping of God's ordinance and the avoiding of the abominable customs (the obedience and the purity) — the holiness (the people kept pure); 'defile not yourselves therein: I am the LORD your God' (the purity required, sealed by the covenant name, vv.2, the figure, the holiness grounded in the covenant God, 1 Peter 1:16, the providence, the chapter closed with the divine seal, the call to holiness) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: Therefore shall ye keep mine ordinance, that ye commit not any one of these abominable customs, which were committed before you, and that ye defile not yourselves therein: I am the LORD your God
the closing charge to keep God's ordinance and not defile themselves
the people kept pure from the abominable customs of the nations, the obedience grounded in the covenant God
the seal of the divine name closing the chapter (vv.2; 1 Peter 1:16)
